skill.name: security-gate
skill.description: Review input handling, authorization, secrets, and common web vulnerabilities before release.
skill.summary: Run a guided security gate before shipping application changes.
skill.author: DanielPodolsky

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk strings, but they appear inside Markdown checklist text and fenced examples. No executable files, command launchers, network calls, or prompt injection attempts were found in the reviewed skill content.

low_findings.title: Static Code-Execution Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The eval, new Function, and innerHTML detections are checklist or example text warning users against unsafe patterns. They are not executable skill code and do not create runtime code execution or XSS by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged strings appear in Markdown checklist items and fenced educational examples. No JavaScript file or callable skill handler executes these patterns.

low_findings.title: Command and System-Reconnaissance Hits Are Markdown Formatting False Positives
low_findings.description: Backtick and reconnaissance detections occur in fenced response templates, SQL examples, or placeholder file locations. They do not instruct the agent to run shell commands or collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are inside Markdown code fences and sample review output. The skill contains no shell execution mechanism or tool-use instruction to execute them.

low_findings.title: Secret and Sensitive-File Detections Are Safe Review Guidance
low_findings.description: The process.env API key example teaches storing secrets outside source code. Windows SAM detections on response-template lines are false positives with no matching sensitive-file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable reference is a safe example, and the cited SAM lines contain response headings rather than file paths. I did not find evidence of credential access or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Detection Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hit on the description line does not correspond to a cryptographic algorithm or implementation. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, DES, or related weak crypto usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is front matter describing the skill purpose. It contains no cryptographic API, algorithm name, or executable implementation.

content.value_statement: Security gaps are often found too late in the release process. This skill provides a structured gate for checking input handling, access control, secrets, and common vulnerabilities.
actual_capabilities: Asks targeted questions about user input, data access, and secret handling.
Guides review of authentication, authorization, and sensitive data exposure.
Checks common web risks such as SQL injection, XSS, IDOR, and CSRF.
Classifies review outcomes as pass, warning, or critical warning.
Provides response templates for clear security review feedback.
Encourages Socratic questions that help developers reason about risk.
limitations: It is a review guide, not an automated vulnerability scanner.
It does not run tests, inspect dependencies, or verify deployed systems.
It relies on the assistant and user to inspect the relevant code paths.
It gives warnings and questions, not complete remediation plans.

use_cases.title: Pre-release feature review
use_cases.description: Run the gate before merging a feature that accepts user input or exposes data.

use_cases.title: Security coaching during code review
use_cases.description: Use the guided questions to help contributors identify authorization and validation gaps.

use_cases.title: Lightweight product risk check
use_cases.description: Confirm whether a feature handles secrets, personal data, and access rules before launch.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic security gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security-gate skill to review this feature before release. Focus on input validation, authorization, and exposed secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Review a new endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the security gate to this endpoint. Identify where input enters, what data is accessed, and who should be allowed to access it.

prompt_templates.title: Check sensitive data handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Feature touches tokens, accounts, or private records
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security gate to review how this change stores, logs, returns, and protects sensitive data.

prompt_templates.title: Perform a release readiness review
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a full security gate for this release. Give a pass, warning, or critical warning with evidence and follow-up questions.

output_examples.input: Review a profile update feature that accepts display names and avatar uploads.
output_examples.output: Security gate warning: validate file type, size, and content. Confirm display names are escaped before rendering. Verify users can update only their own profile.

output_examples.input: Review an admin-only billing report endpoint.
output_examples.output: Security gate warning: confirm admin role checks on the server. Ensure responses exclude unnecessary customer fields and logs do not include payment details.

output_examples.input: Review a password reset flow before release.
output_examples.output: Security gate critical warning: verify token expiration, single-use behavior, and rate limits. Check that errors do not reveal whether an account exists.

best_practices: Run the gate before merge when a change touches input, identity, permissions, or secrets.
Ask for concrete file locations and evidence for each warning.
Treat critical warnings as release blockers until the risk is resolved.
anti_patterns: Using the gate as a replacement for automated security tests.
Accepting vague answers without checking the relevant code path.
Ignoring authorization because authentication already exists.

faq.question: Does this skill scan code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a structured review process and checklist for an assistant-led security review.

faq.question: What types of issues does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on input validation, authorization, secret handling, data exposure, and common web vulnerabilities.

faq.question: Can it replace a penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It is useful before release, but it does not replace expert testing or production monitoring.

faq.question: Why does it use warnings instead of blocking every issue?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for coaching and review. Teams can decide which warnings must block release.

faq.question: Is it suitable for non-web systems?
faq.answer: Partly. The questions are broadly useful, but many examples focus on web application security.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
